summary: The sprint's performance profile is mostly acceptable. The biggest concern is the dividend upsert loop in kis_dividend.py, which executes one DB statement per row inside a loop without batching — for a user with 50 holdings each returning 10 dividend rows, this is 500 sequential DB round-trips. The benchmark_delta endpoint loads all PriceSnapshot rows for all tickers across the period with no pagination or row limit, which can become a large query for portfolios with many tickers over long periods. The AnalysisSection component fires 3 independent API queries on every mount (portfolio-history, benchmark-delta, fx-gain-loss) with no loading skeletons, causing cumulative waterfall layout shifts. The OrderDialogProvider adds a portfolios query that runs on every dashboard page load even when the user never opens an order dialog.

findings.title: Dividend upsert loop: N sequential DB round-trips, no batching
findings.detail: upsert_dividends iterates rows one by one, executing a separate INSERT ... ON CONFLICT statement per row. For a typical run with 30 tickers × 5 dividend records = 150 sequential DB calls per scheduler invocation. PostgreSQL supports bulk upsert using VALUES lists with a single ON CONFLICT clause. The current approach also calls db.commit() inside the function for every batch passed by the caller, creating unnecessary commit overhead. Estimated impact: 150 x ~2ms round-trip = ~300ms added latency per scheduler run, plus connection pool pressure.
findings.fix: Rewrite upsert_dividends to build a single INSERT ... VALUES (...), (...) ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE statement using SQLAlchemy's insert().values(list_of_dicts). Call db.commit() once at the end. This reduces N DB round-trips to 1 per batch.

findings.title: benchmark_delta loads all PriceSnapshot rows for all tickers without a row limit
findings.detail: The snap_query selects all PriceSnapshot rows for all user tickers from start_date onward, with no LIMIT. For a user with 20 tickers over a 1-year period (250 trading days), this is 5,000 rows loaded into memory as Python objects in date_ticker_map. The query also lacks an index hint for the (ticker, snapshot_date) composite scan. As portfolios grow this will become a measurable slow query.
findings.fix: Add `.limit(10000)` as a safety cap. Ensure the PriceSnapshot table has a composite index on (ticker, snapshot_date). Consider aggregating in SQL using a window function or GROUP BY instead of loading all rows into Python memory.

findings.title: AnalysisSection fires 3 independent queries on mount with no loading state
findings.detail: Three useQuery calls (portfolio-history, benchmark-delta, fx-gain-loss) are all independent with no skeleton/loading UI. Each resolves at different times causing incremental layout shifts in the portfolio detail page. The benchmark-delta query does not include portfolioId in the queryKey or params, so it shares cache with the same query in dashboard/page.tsx — but if the cache is cold, it adds a third concurrent request. The fx-gain-loss query has staleTime=10min which means it may show stale data from a different portfolio context.
findings.fix: Add loading skeleton placeholders for each card while queries are pending. Use Promise.all or React Suspense boundaries to batch the visual update. Consider a single aggregated analytics endpoint to reduce request count.

findings.title: OrderDialogProvider eagerly fetches portfolios on every dashboard mount
findings.detail: The portfolios query runs immediately on mount with staleTime=60_000. This adds one background API request on every dashboard page load, even for users who never visit a stock detail page or click Buy/Sell. While staleTime=60s prevents excessive refetching, the initial cold request adds to first-load API waterfall.
findings.fix: Consider lazy-initializing the query only when the first 'the-wealth:order' event fires (i.e., use `enabled: false` and call `refetch()` on event). Or rely on the existing ['portfolios'] cache key that is likely already populated by the portfolios list page.
